question: Explain the core principle behind a greedy algorithm. Provide one key advantage and one key limitation of this design technique.
expectedPoints: A greedy algorithm makes the locally optimal choice at each stage with the hope of finding a global optimum.
Advantage: Often simple to implement and highly efficient.
Limitation: Does not always yield the globally optimal solution and can be short-sighted.

question: Compare and contrast Dynamic Programming and Divide and Conquer paradigms. Use a specific example algorithm for each to illustrate your points.
expectedPoints: Both break problems into smaller subproblems.
Divide and Conquer solves subproblems independently (e.g., Merge Sort), often leading to recursive solutions.
Dynamic Programming solves overlapping subproblems only once and stores the results (memoization/tabulation) for reuse (e.g., Fibonacci using DP).
DP is typically applied to optimization problems with optimal substructure and overlapping subproblems.

question: Walk through the steps of the Bellman-Ford algorithm for the single-source shortest path problem. Explain how it detects negative-weight cycles and analyze its time complexity.
expectedPoints: The algorithm initializes the distance to the source as 0 and all other vertices as infinity.
It then relaxes all edges |V| - 1 times, where |V| is the number of vertices, guaranteeing the shortest path is found if no negative cycles exist.
To detect a negative cycle, it relaxes all edges one more time; if any distance can be improved, a negative cycle exists.
The time complexity is O(|V| * |E|), as it performs |V| - 1 passes over all |E| edges, plus one additional pass for cycle detection.
